title: SQL for Analytics

commands.name: SELECT and FROM
commands.summary: Project specific columns and expressions from source tables.
commands.syntax:
SELECT column_list
FROM table_name;
commands.description: Project specific columns and expressions from source tables. Use it for building the base dataset for analysis before applying joins, filters, or aggregations and curating a clean reporting extract with only required fields. Key parameters: column_list, table_name.

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description: Inputs that control how SELECT and FROM is applied.
pitfalls: Confirm table names and SQL dialect behavior before production deployment.

returns.description: Relational output that can feed dashboards, analyses, and downstream SQL logic.

examples.title: SELECT and FROM primary workflow
examples.scenario: SELECT and FROM is used in a reusable business analytics workflow.
examples.code:
WITH orders AS (
  SELECT 1 AS order_id, 'West' AS region, 120.0 AS revenue, DATE '2026-01-01' AS order_date
  UNION ALL
  SELECT 2, 'East', 90.0, DATE '2026-01-02'
)
-- Apply SELECT and FROM
SELECT column_list
FROM table_name;
examples.expectedOutput: A valid output shape that can be adapted to production tables/files.
walkthrough: Create a toy dataset that mirrors your project columns and value types.
Apply SELECT and FROM with explicit parameters shown in the snippet, then verify output shape and values.
Transfer the same pattern to your full dataset and rerun validation checks.

examples.title: SELECT and FROM alternate business slice
examples.scenario: Second realistic usage variant for SELECT and FROM on another dataset slice.
examples.code:
WITH sessions AS (
  SELECT 'A' AS segment, 12 AS visits, DATE '2026-02-01' AS day
  UNION ALL
  SELECT 'B', 20, DATE '2026-02-02'
)
-- Reuse SELECT and FROM for another business slice
SELECT column_list
FROM table_name;
examples.expectedOutput: A second executable pattern for transfer to another reporting context.
walkthrough: Create a toy dataset that mirrors your project columns and value types.
Apply SELECT and FROM with explicit parameters shown in the snippet, then verify output shape and values.
Transfer the same pattern to your full dataset and rerun validation checks.

notes: Validate on a small CTE first, then scale to warehouse tables.
commands.overview: Project specific columns and expressions from source tables. Use SELECT and FROM when building the base dataset for analysis before applying joins, filters, or aggregations. Typical business pattern: curating a clean reporting extract with only required fields. Key parameters: column_list, table_name. Returns result set with shape rows that satisfy the query scope with selected columns.
applications: Primary application: SELECT and FROM is used for curating a clean reporting extract with only required fields.
Workflow application: use this as the first query stage, then layer filtering, joins, and aggregations.
Delivery application: document chosen parameters and output checks so teammates can reproduce results.

commands.name: DISTINCT
commands.summary: Return unique values or unique row combinations for selected columns.
commands.syntax:
SELECT DISTINCT column_list
FROM table_name;
commands.description: Return unique values or unique row combinations for selected columns. Use it for checking cardinality and removing duplicate combinations in exploratory queries and profiling unique customer IDs, regions, or product categories. Key parameters: column_list, table_name.

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description: Inputs that control how DISTINCT is applied.
pitfalls: Confirm table names and SQL dialect behavior before production deployment.

returns.description: Relational output that can feed dashboards, analyses, and downstream SQL logic.

  UNION ALL
  SELECT 2, 'East', 90.0, DATE '2026-01-02'
)
-- Apply DISTINCT

FROM table_name;

commands.name: WHERE

parameters.name: inputs

commands.name: ORDER BY

commands.description: Sort query output by one or more expressions. Use it for ranking entities and making output deterministic for review or export and sorting top customers by revenue or newest records by timestamp. Key parameters: sort_expression, sort_direction.

commands.name: LIMIT and OFFSET
commands.summary: Return a bounded slice of rows for sampling or pagination.
commands.description: Return a bounded slice of rows for sampling or pagination. Use it for previewing data quickly and paginating large result sets and inspecting the first n ranked rows during query development. Key parameters: n, m.

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description: Inputs that control how LIMIT and OFFSET is applied.
pitfalls: Confirm table names and SQL dialect behavior before production deployment.

returns.description: Relational output that can feed dashboards, analyses, and downstream SQL logic.

  UNION ALL
  SELECT 2, 'East', 90.0, DATE '2026-01-02'
)
-- Apply LIMIT and OFFSET

FROM table_name

commands.name: LIKE and ILIKE
commands.summary: Match text values with wildcard patterns using case-sensitive or insensitive search.
commands.description: Match text values with wildcard patterns using case-sensitive or insensitive search. Use it for filtering free-text columns for prefixes, suffixes, or contains patterns and finding campaign names containing promo terms or channel tags. Key parameters: text_column, pattern.

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description: Inputs that control how LIKE and ILIKE is applied.
pitfalls: Confirm table names and SQL dialect behavior before production deployment.

returns.description: Relational output that can feed dashboards, analyses, and downstream SQL logic.

  UNION ALL
  SELECT 2, 'East', 90.0, DATE '2026-01-02'
)
-- Apply LIKE and ILIKE

FROM table_name
WHERE text_column LIKE 'pattern%';

commands.name: IN and BETWEEN
commands.summary: Filter by membership lists and inclusive numeric/date ranges.
commands.description: Filter by membership lists and inclusive numeric/date ranges. Use it for applying explicit allowlists and bounded thresholds in business logic and selecting target regions and transactions within approved value ranges. Key parameters: value_list, range_bounds.

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description: Inputs that control how IN and BETWEEN is applied.

  UNION ALL
  SELECT 2, 'East', 90.0, DATE '2026-01-02'
)
-- Apply IN and BETWEEN

FROM table_name
WHERE value IN (a, b, c) OR metric BETWEEN low AND high;

commands.name: HAVING
commands.summary: Filter grouped results using aggregate conditions.
commands.syntax:
SELECT dimension, COUNT(*) AS n
FROM table_name
GROUP BY dimension
HAVING COUNT(*) >= threshold;
commands.description: Filter grouped results using aggregate conditions. Use it for keeping only groups that meet minimum volume or threshold rules and retaining segments with at least a target count of events. Key parameters: aggregate_condition, threshold.

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description: Inputs that control how HAVING is applied.
pitfalls: Confirm table names and SQL dialect behavior before production deployment.

returns.description: Relational output that can feed dashboards, analyses, and downstream SQL logic.

  UNION ALL
  SELECT 2, 'East', 90.0, DATE '2026-01-02'
)
-- Apply HAVING
SELECT dimension, COUNT(*) AS n
FROM table_name
GROUP BY dimension
HAVING COUNT(*) >= threshold;

commands.name: COUNT, SUM, AVG

commands.syntax:

commands.name: CASE WHEN
commands.summary: Encode conditional business rules into labeled categories.
commands.syntax:
SELECT CASE WHEN condition THEN 'A' ELSE 'B' END AS bucket
FROM table_name;

commands.name: COALESCE and NULLIF
commands.summary: Standardize missing or sentinel values with null-handling helpers.
commands.syntax:
SELECT COALESCE(metric, 0), NULLIF(text_col, "")
FROM table_name;
commands.description: Standardize missing or sentinel values with null-handling helpers. Use it for cleaning null-heavy extracts before aggregation or joins and replacing null metrics with defaults and blank strings with NULL. Key parameters: metric, fallback_value.

parameters.name: inputs

  UNION ALL

)
-- Apply COALESCE and NULLIF

commands.name: INNER JOIN
commands.summary: Join two tables and keep only matching keys from both sides.

commands.name: LAG and LEAD
commands.summary: Access previous or next row values within an ordered partition.
commands.syntax:
SELECT ts, metric, LAG(metric) OVER (PARTITION BY key ORDER BY ts) AS prev_metric
FROM table_name;
commands.description: Access previous or next row values within an ordered partition. Use it for calculating period-over-period changes and sequential comparisons and computing day-over-day deltas in traffic, revenue, or conversion metrics. Key parameters: metric, ordering_column, partition_key.

-- Reuse LAG and LEAD for another business slice

commands.name: PARTITION BY
commands.summary: Scope window calculations independently within each segment.
commands.syntax:
SELECT *, SUM(metric) OVER (PARTITION BY segment) AS segment_total
FROM table_name;
commands.description: Scope window calculations independently within each segment. Use it for computing running metrics without collapsing row-level detail and calculating per-segment totals or moving stats while keeping original rows. Key parameters: partition_key, window_function.

parameters.name: inputs

commands.name: DATE_TRUNC
commands.summary: Bucket timestamps to a fixed calendar grain such as day, week, or month.
commands.description: Bucket timestamps to a fixed calendar grain such as day, week, or month. Use it for creating consistent time buckets for trend and cohort reporting and rolling event-level data into weekly KPI tables. Key parameters: timestamp_col, granularity.

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description: Inputs that control how DATE_TRUNC is applied.
pitfalls: Confirm table names and SQL dialect behavior before production deployment.

returns.description: Relational output that can feed dashboards, analyses, and downstream SQL logic.

examples.code:
WITH orders AS (
  SELECT 1 AS order_id, 'West' AS region, 120.0 AS revenue, DATE '2026-01-01' AS order_date
  UNION ALL
  SELECT 2, 'East', 90.0, DATE '2026-01-02'
)
-- Apply DATE_TRUNC

FROM table_name
GROUP BY 1;

commands.name: Date Difference
commands.summary: Measure elapsed time between two dates or timestamps.
commands.syntax:
SELECT EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (end_ts - start_ts)) / 3600 AS hours_between
FROM table_name;
commands.description: Measure elapsed time between two dates or timestamps. Use it for analyzing latency, cycle time, and SLA performance and tracking fulfillment or response-time intervals across workflows. Key parameters: start_ts, end_ts, unit.

commands.name: String Functions
commands.summary: Normalize and parse text fields with string transformations.
commands.syntax:
